metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC1's vague oracle 'docs link/section check passes' with a concrete grep-based proving command that anchors on each required section heading (e.g. `grep -qE '^## .*(static|jwt|introspection|proxy|custom)' docs/mcp-auth.md` for verifier modes, plus analogous checks for security model, behind-a-proxy, connection paths, and residual risks).
evidence: "observable oracle: the file exists and a docs link/section check passes"

AC1 names ~8 distinct required sub-sections (5 verifier modes, security model, behind-a-proxy, 3 connection paths, residual risks) but provides no grep or script to assert each heading/section exists in the generated file.
findings.finding: AC1's observable oracle — 'the file exists and a docs link/section check passes' — is not a concrete, executable proving command. An agent cannot know what tool or script performs 'a docs link/section check,' so completeness of the required sub-sections (all five verifier modes with examples, three connection paths, nginx/ALB example, residual risks section, etc.) cannot be mechanically verified.
scenarios: An agent delivers a file that exists and contains some of the required sections but omits, e.g., the nginx/ALB example or one verifier mode — the vague oracle passes silently.
A CI job could in principle check section headers but no such job or command is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a shell one-liner or a small `scripts/check_mcp_auth_doc.sh` (invoked from `make lint`) that greps for the required H2/H3 headings by name and exits non-zero if any are absent. Reference this script as the proving command in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC2 that verifies required ADR content (e.g. `grep -q 'GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f' docs/adr/<filename>.md && grep -q 'PVE-2026-93393' docs/adr/<filename>.md` for CVE citations, plus checks for 'resource-server' and 'deferred' headings).
evidence: "An ADR file exists under docs/adr/ recording: the resource-server-only + build-own decision, the composite audience choke point, the five modes, the transport-hardening CVE citations, and the deferred items."
No proving command is stated for AC2; the observable oracle is entirely absent.
findings.finding: AC2 has no concrete proving command for content completeness — only file existence is implied. The ADR must record the resource-server-only decision, composite audience choke point, five modes, specific CVE citations, and deferred items, but there is no grep or script to verify any of this content is actually present.
scenarios: Agent creates the ADR file with only a title and context section, omitting CVE citations or deferred items — AC2 passes trivially on 'file exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the target ADR filename (or numbering convention to follow) explicitly in the plan, e.g. 'follow the existing `docs/adr/NNNN-<slug>.md` pattern; use the next available sequence number at time of authoring.'
evidence: "A new ADR under docs/adr/ recording: the resource-server-only decision..."
No filename, number, or pointer to an existing naming convention (e.g. 'ADR-0044', 'adr-mcp-auth.md') is given.
findings.finding: The ADR filename and numbering scheme are unspecified — the plan says only 'a new ADR under docs/adr/' — leaving the executor to guess the next sequential number or naming convention, which is a defaultable gap that should be stated explicitly.
scenarios: Executor picks a number that conflicts with a concurrently authored ADR, or uses a different filename format than the project convention, requiring a rename.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'ADR filename: `docs/adr/NNNN-mcp-auth-resource-server.md` where NNNN is the next available number in the sequence at merge time; follow the existing ADR header template.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to S7's scope/AC to audit existing docs (README.md, CLAUDE.md, any transport or deployment guides) for content invalidated by the new MCP auth surface, and update or cross-link them to `docs/mcp-auth.md`.
evidence: Scope states: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only'
AC3 names only mcp-reference.md, env-vars.md, and server.json as consistency targets
ABSENCE: no mention of README.md, CLAUDE.md, or other existing prose docs that may describe HTTP transport setup or auth absence
findings.finding: The plan commits to regenerating `docs/mcp-reference.md`, `docs/env-vars.md`, and `server.json` for consistency, but does not include an audit of other existing documentation (e.g., README.md transport/configuration sections, CLAUDE.md developer-setup instructions, any existing getting-started or deployment guides) that S1–S6 may have invalidated by introducing a new auth layer and HTTP transport configuration surface.
findings.location: Scope section and AC line 3 ('docs/mcp-reference.md and docs/env-vars.md are fully regenerated…')
scenarios: A developer reads the existing README transport section (which may say 'no auth supported') and follows stale instructions, bypassing the new auth layer
CLAUDE.md dev-setup steps omit the new env vars, causing contributors to run the server without auth in local dev and misunderstand the security posture
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' list: 'Audit README.md and CLAUDE.md for existing HTTP-transport or MCP-server-configuration content; update or add a cross-reference to docs/mcp-auth.md where stale.' Add a corresponding AC line: 'README.md and CLAUDE.md contain no transport/auth guidance that contradicts the new auth model, or explicitly cross-reference docs/mcp-auth.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a **Troubleshooting / error-state** section to the `docs/mcp-auth.md` outline (and corresponding AC) covering: token-rejection diagnosis (which verifier failed, what the client sees), IdP-unreachable behavior during introspection, `auth_resource_server_url` validation errors, and allowlist-blocked-request symptoms.
evidence: Plan outline lists: enabling HTTP+auth; five verifier modes; security model; behind-proxy deployment; three interactive-client connection paths; residual risks — no troubleshooting/error-state section
AC1 observable oracle is 'the file exists and a docs link/section check passes' — no requirement that error/misconfiguration paths are covered
'tokens/secrets never logged' (security model bullet) reduces ambient debuggability, heightening the need for explicit operator error guidance
Three client connection paths described are all success paths (static bearer works, OAuth with DCR works, pre-registered client works)
findings.finding: The `docs/mcp-auth.md` outline covers happy-path operator flows (enabling auth, five verifier mode configs, three client connection paths) but does not specify any troubleshooting or error-diagnosis guidance for operators — e.g., what a client sees when a token fails validation, how to diagnose an unreachable IdP during introspection, how to identify an `auth_resource_server_url` misconfiguration, or how to tell whether the allowlist is blocking a legitimate request. The plan notes 'tokens/secrets never logged,' which further reduces ambient debuggability, making explicit error-state guidance essential.
findings.location: docs/mcp-auth.md outline (What section) and AC item 1
scenarios: Operator sets `auth_resource_server_url` to an http:// URL on a non-loopback bind — server rejects it, operator has no documented diagnosis path
IdP endpoint is unreachable during introspection — client receives 401, operator cannot tell whether the issue is network, config, or token
`http_allowed_hosts` blocks a legitimate reverse-proxy request — request silently dropped with no operator-visible error guidance
Token is rejected by the composite audience check — client gets 401 with a WWW-Authenticate header, but operator doesn't know which verifier failed or why
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `docs/mcp-auth.md` outline in the plan's What section with a 'Troubleshooting' subsection listing: (1) reading the WWW-Authenticate header to identify the failed verifier; (2) expected log lines (at INFO/WARN) emitted on auth failure (without leaking tokens); (3) common misconfigurations (`auth_resource_server_url` http vs https, missing allowlist entry) and their observable symptoms; (4) how to verify the PRM is served correctly (`/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`). Update AC1 to require this section exists with at least these four topics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make AC3 explicit about its dependency: state that S7 MUST run on a branch that includes S1-S6's merged auth keys, and add a named integration check (e.g., a grep asserting `auth_resource_server_url` and at least one verifier-mode key appear in `docs/env-vars.md` or `docs/mcp-reference.md`) so the drift gate cannot pass on an empty auth surface.
evidence: Plan quote: 'each key-adding story S1–S6 already registers ITS keys and regenerates its slice; S7 confirms the integrated whole is consistent'
Zero grep hits for: auth_strategies, auth_resource_server_url, required_scopes, http_allowed_hosts, http_allowed_origins, static_headers, resource_metadata, WWW-Authenticate in the codebase
Current mcp_server.py MCP_ENV_VARS has 4 entries only (REBAR_ROOT, REBAR_MCP_READONLY, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC) — no auth keys
No S1-S6 branch artifacts, modules, or config schema entries for MCP auth are present in src/rebar/
findings.finding: The plan's pivotal sequencing premise — 'each key-adding story S1–S6 already registers ITS keys and regenerates its slice' — is an empirically-checkable codebase claim (S1-S6 must have landed auth config keys, verifier modules, and reference regeneration before S7 runs) but zero auth infrastructure exists in the current tree: `auth_strategies`, `auth_resource_server_url`, `required_scopes`, `http_allowed_hosts`, `http_allowed_origins`, `static_headers`, the five verifier modes, `resource_metadata` URL serving, and `WWW-Authenticate` all return no search hits. S7's entire consistency-check AC (AC3) rests on this premise.
findings.location: Why section / 'S1–S6 already registers ITS keys and regenerates its slice'; AC3
scenarios: If S1-S6 are not yet merged/complete when S7 is executed, `docs/env-vars.md` and `docs/mcp-reference.md` will not contain any auth keys, making AC3's 'fully regenerated for the integrated new-key set' vacuously false without any gate failure.

If S1-S6 used a non-standard env-read pattern not recognized by gen_env_registry.py's KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS, new auth env vars would silently not appear in env-vars.md, and S7's consistency check would pass on an incomplete document.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Pre-condition: branch includes S1–S6 merges. Verify by asserting `auth_resource_server_url` (or an agreed sentinel auth key) appears in the regenerated `docs/env-vars.md` — e.g., `grep -q auth_resource_server_url docs/env-vars.md` — so the drift gate cannot trivially pass on a pre-S1-S6 tree.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add to scope) that any new env-read helpers introduced in S1-S6 for auth config are added to `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` in `gen_env_registry.py`, and name this as an explicit S7 verification step.
evidence: docs/env-vars.md line 5: 'reads through other indirections are not captured — extend KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS in the generator to cover a new helper'
Zero codebase evidence of how S1-S6 auth config keys will be read (no auth modules exist yet)
Plan makes no assertion about extending KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS for auth vars
findings.finding: The plan asserts `docs/env-vars.md` will be 'fully regenerated for the integrated new-key set' of auth config, but `gen_env_registry.py` only captures env vars read through recognized patterns (`KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`). The plan does not verify that new auth config keys (e.g., `auth_resource_server_url`, `required_scopes`) are read via those recognized helpers — if they are read through a new helper or config layer not in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`, they will be silently absent from `docs/env-vars.md` and the drift gate will still pass.
findings.location: What section / 'Final reference/manifest consistency check'; AC3
scenarios: Auth config reads go through a new config layer (e.g., a Pydantic settings class or a new _rebar_auth_env helper) not in KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS — gen_env_registry.py silently misses them, env-vars.md is incomplete, but the drift gate still passes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` (Operator section) to the plan's edit targets to index the new `docs/mcp-auth.md`, preventing the `check_docs_index` CI gate from failing.
evidence: scripts/check_docs_index.py find_unindexed() globs docs_dir.glob('*.md') and reports any top-level doc not linked from docs/README.md
tests/unit/test_check_docs_index.py::test_current_repo_index_is_clean asserts chk.main(['--check']) == 0, which will fail if mcp-auth.md is unindexed
.github/workflows/test.yml line 276: 'python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check' is part of make test / CI
docs/README.md Operator section lists every operator doc explicitly; mcp-auth.md is absent from the plan's edit targets
Plan scope states only: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only'
findings.finding: Creating docs/mcp-auth.md without updating docs/README.md will fail the docs-index CI gate (scripts/check_docs_index.py --check, wired into make test) because that gate requires every top-level docs/*.md to be linked from docs/README.md.
findings.location: Scope section / AC4 ('make lint && make test pass')
scenarios: Developer creates docs/mcp-auth.md per the plan, runs make test, and the docs-index gate fails with 'unindexed doc (not linked from README.md): mcp-auth.md'
CI Verified vote is blocked on the unenumerated docs/README.md edit
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Update `docs/README.md` Operator section to link `mcp-auth.md` — required to pass the `scripts/check_docs_index.py` drift gate.' Add a corresponding AC bullet: '`docs/README.md` Operator section links `docs/mcp-auth.md` and the docs-index gate passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC or Scope note requiring `docs/README.md` to be updated with an entry for `docs/mcp-auth.md` under the Operator section, and name `scripts/check_docs_index.py` as the observable oracle for AC1 (replacing the vague 'docs link/section check passes').
evidence: scripts/check_docs_index.py: 'find_unindexed' returns every top-level docs/*.md not linked from docs/README.md and main() returns exit code 1
.github/workflows/test.yml: 'Docs-index completeness gate' runs python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check in CI
AC1 oracle: 'the file exists and a docs link/section check passes' — does not name the gate or require a docs/README.md update
Plan scope section: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only' — no mention of docs/README.md
findings.finding: The plan creates docs/mcp-auth.md but does not explicitly require updating docs/README.md to add an index entry for it. The existing docs-index completeness gate (scripts/check_docs_index.py, wired into CI) fails when any top-level docs/*.md is not linked from docs/README.md — so an implementer who creates the file but skips the README entry will encounter a CI failure the plan did not prepare them for. AC1's oracle phrase 'a docs link/section check passes' gestures at this but does not name the gate or the required action.
findings.location: AC line 1 (observable oracle); Approach & rationale section
scenarios: Implementer creates docs/mcp-auth.md (satisfying the file-exists check) and runs the regeneration scripts successfully, but does not add a docs/README.md entry. CI's docs-index completeness gate fires, blocking the merge on what the plan implied was a purely-docs leaf story.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, append: 'and `docs/README.md` is updated with a link to `mcp-auth.md` under the Operator section (observable oracle: `python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` exits 0)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `make typecheck` to S7 AC4 (change to `make lint && make typecheck && make test`) so the final integrated branch satisfies the parent epic's explicit requirement that all three gates pass together.
evidence: Parent epic final AC: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the final integrated branch.'
S7 AC4: '`make lint && make test` pass (docs/mechanical leaf; no typecheck-relevant code added here).'

S7 is explicitly sequenced last ('Documentation-and-ADR story sequenced last so it documents the *actually shipped* surface (S1–S6)'), making it the natural owner of the integrated-branch typecheck gate.

The rationalization 'no typecheck-relevant code added here' addresses S7's own additions but does not absolve the final integration story from verifying that the full integrated branch, including S1–S6 type annotations and imports, passes typecheck.
findings.finding: S7's AC4 drops `make typecheck` from its close gate, rationalizing that 'no typecheck-relevant code added here.' But S7 is the last story in the epic, and the parent's final AC explicitly requires '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the final integrated branch.' Since no other child story owns typecheck for the integrated branch, dropping it from S7's AC means the parent's integrated-branch typecheck gate is never formally claimed or verified at close.
findings.location: S7 AC4 / parent epic (3393-83ca-5501-4e6e) final Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: S7 closes without running typecheck. A type error introduced by S1–S6 goes undetected until a future CI run or a separate audit, violating the parent's integrated-branch gate.

The epic is marked complete with all child stories closed, but the parent's 'make typecheck passes on the final integrated branch' AC is technically unfulfilled because the story that ran the final integrated verification explicitly excluded typecheck.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4 to: '`make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass on the integrated branch (S7 adds no new type-annotated code, so typecheck is expected to be fast; the AC nevertheless gates on it because S7 is the final story and the parent epic's AC requires typecheck to pass on the combined branch).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that PVE-2026-93393 is a real, lookupable advisory (or replace it with its correct CVE/GHSA identifier); if it was a placeholder, substitute the actual audience-confusion advisory or remove the parenthetical and ground the choke-point motivation in the RFC 8707 resource-indicator spec alone.
evidence: Plan text: 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
Search across entire codebase returns zero matches for 'PVE-2026-93393'
PVE- is not a prefix used by CVE, GHSA (GitHub Security Advisories), or OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities) databases
findings.finding: PVE-2026-93393 uses a non-standard identifier prefix ('PVE-') that does not correspond to any known CVE, GHSA, or OSV namespace. The plan cites it as the prior-art motivating the composite-audience choke-point design — a load-bearing architectural justification. If this identifier is fictitious or erroneous, the stated motivation for the composite loses its grounding.
findings.location: What section — composite audience choke point bullet: 'the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393'
scenarios: An operator or security auditor looks up PVE-2026-93393 and finds it does not exist, undermining confidence in the ADR's cited motivations.
The composite choke-point design is challenged in a security review because its stated prior-art precedent cannot be corroborated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify each of the three transport-hardening identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124) against a public advisory database, replace any that are incorrect with their real identifiers, and remove or restate any that have no real counterpart.
evidence: Plan text: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
search_files for GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f: zero codebase matches
search_files for CVE-2026-40575: zero codebase matches
search_files for CVE-2026-27124: zero codebase matches
CVE-2026-* identifiers are future-dated; no 2026 CVEs have been published in real databases at any point before mid-2025
findings.finding: The three transport-hardening CVE identifiers cited in the ADR section (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124) cannot be verified: no matches exist anywhere in the codebase, and the 2026-year CVEs are future-dated relative to any published CVE database. These identifiers are unverified capability assertions in a plan whose stated purpose is auditability by a security reviewer.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet: 'the transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
scenarios: A security auditor looks up the three cited advisories and finds none exist, invalidating the stated transport-hardening rationale.
The ADR is cited in a compliance review; the nonexistent CVE references cause the entire ADR to be flagged as unreliable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'CIMD' with the correct standard term (likely RFC 7591 Dynamic Client Registration, or CIBA if backchannel auth is intended) and verify the claimed IdP support (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta) against each IdP's published capability list.
evidence: Plan text: 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
Search for 'CIMD' and 'Client-Initiated Metadata' in codebase: zero matches
CIMD is not defined in RFC 6749, RFC 7591, RFC 8414, RFC 9126, RFC 9156, or the MCP spec
findings.finding: The plan uses 'CIMD' as a parenthetical descriptor for a client connection path ('automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'), but 'CIMD' is not a standard OAuth/OIDC abbreviation. The closest standard terms are RFC 7591 Dynamic Client Registration (DCR) and CIBA (Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication, RFC 9156). An operator following the guide to configure path (2) cannot resolve what protocol 'CIMD' refers to.
findings.location: What section — three interactive-client connection paths: '(2) automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
scenarios: An operator deploying with Keycloak tries to look up 'CIMD' support and finds no such protocol, leaving them unable to configure path (2).
The documentation is read by a security reviewer who flags the unexplained acronym as an unresolved reference.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'DCR or CIMD' with 'DCR (RFC 7591) or CIBA (RFC 9156)' and confirm which of the listed IdPs support each protocol, as Okta and Entra have partial DCR support that may not cover anonymous registration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify Claude Code CLI flag names (`--header`, `--client-id`, `--client-secret`) and Claude.ai config key (`static_headers`) against official Claude Code `--help` output / Anthropic MCP client documentation before authoring the operator guide examples, and cite the source version.
evidence: Plan text: 'static bearer via Claude Code `--header "Authorization: Bearer …"` / Claude.ai `static_headers`'
Plan text: 'pre-registered `--client-id`/`--client-secret` for IdPs without anonymous DCR'
No `--header`, `--client-id`, or `--client-secret` references found anywhere in the repo codebase — cannot cross-check
Evidence class: Unverified (flag-level empiricism not performed; cannot run `claude --help`)
findings.finding: The operator guide prescribes exact Claude Code CLI flags (`--header "Authorization: Bearer …"` and `--client-id`/`--client-secret`) that operators will copy verbatim; these flag names are unverified against the actual `claude --help` output and a flag-name mismatch causes silent runtime failures after shipping the doc.
findings.location: What > docs/mcp-auth.md > 'The three interactive-client connection paths'
scenarios: An operator copies the `--header` example verbatim, the flag is actually `--add-header` or injected via a config file, and the token is silently ignored — auth bypass in practice.
The `static_headers` Claude.ai config key is renamed or structured differently in the actual Claude.ai MCP config schema, breaking the proxy deployment example.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before authoring the ADR, verify each cited advisory ID (PVE-2026-93393, GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124) against its authoritative public database (GHSA, NVD, OSV) and record the verified title + affected package in the plan/ADR; replace any that cannot be verified with a description of the actual observed vulnerability.
evidence: Plan text: 'the composite audience choke point (motivated by the SDK having no `.resource` backstop + the audience-confusion precedent PVE-2026-93393)'
Plan text: 'transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
All four identifiers are 2026-dated — beyond training data cutoff, unverifiable
No matches found in repo for any of these identifiers
Evidence class: Unverified (future-dated; cannot probe NVD, GHSA, or OSV)
findings.finding: The ADR cites four future-dated advisory identifiers (PVE-2026-93393, GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124) as grounding for transport-hardening decisions; these cannot be verified against any public advisory database from training data and may be hallucinated or misattributed identifiers.
findings.location: What > ADR > 'transport hardening (citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f DNS-rebinding, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 forwarded-header, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy)'
scenarios: A security auditor looks up CVE-2026-40575 and finds it does not exist or maps to a completely different vulnerability, undermining the ADR's credibility and triggering a broader audit of the security rationale.
PVE-2026-93393 is a non-standard identifier prefix (PVE ≠ CVE/GHSA/OSV) that may refer to a private tracker or be fabricated — no public database to verify against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the 'CIMD' acronym, cite the spec/RFC it refers to, and confirm that at least one of the named IdPs (Keycloak, Auth0, Okta) actually exposes this capability before including it in the operator guide — or drop it if it cannot be grounded.
evidence: Plan text: 'automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
Zero matches for 'CIMD' anywhere in the repo
DCR = Dynamic Client Registration (RFC 7591) — a real protocol; 'CIMD' expansion is unknown
Evidence class: Unverified — no public spec or IdP documentation reference found
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'CIMD' as a protocol (alongside DCR) that named IdPs (Keycloak, Auth0, Okta) support, enabling 'automatic OAuth' client connection; CIMD is unexpanded and cannot be verified as a real published protocol or confirmed to be supported by the named IdPs.
findings.location: What > docs/mcp-auth.md > 'The three interactive-client connection paths' — path (2)
scenarios: An author writing `docs/mcp-auth.md` copies 'CIMD' into the operator guide; an operator attempts to configure this path and finds no documentation from Keycloak/Auth0/Okta, discovering the guide is unreliable.
The acronym refers to an internal or draft spec not yet published, making the operator guide non-reproducible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that every flat-config example in `docs/mcp-auth.md` for a credential-bearing verifier mode (jwt, introspection, proxy, custom, and the --client-secret client-connection path) MUST use clearly-marked placeholder values AND include an adjacent note directing operators to supply secret fields via environment variable substitution or a secrets manager — not plaintext in config.
evidence: Plan text: 'each of the five verifier modes (`static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom`) with a flat-config example each'
Plan text: 'pre-registered `--client-id`/`--client-secret` for IdPs without anonymous DCR (Entra, Cognito)'

ABSENCE: the plan does not require that flat-config examples use placeholder sentinel values (e.g. `<REPLACE_WITH_SECRET>`) or that each credential field includes a note directing operators to env-var injection / secrets manager

The introspection, proxy, and custom verifier modes inherently involve credential fields (client secrets, upstream auth tokens, signing keys) that should never live in plaintext operator config committed to source control
findings.finding: The plan requires `docs/mcp-auth.md` to include 'a flat-config example each' for all five verifier modes — some of which involve credentials on the boundary-crossing path (JWT signing keys, introspection endpoint client_id/client_secret, proxy credentials, custom verifier auth). The plan does not specify that these config examples must (a) use clearly-marked placeholder values and (b) include inline guidance directing operators to inject secret values via environment variables or a secrets manager rather than storing them in plaintext config. Without this constraint on the doc content, the guide could model insecure configuration practices for a network-exposed auth-critical component.
findings.location: docs/mcp-auth.md section (What bullet 1) — 'each of the five verifier modes with a flat-config example each'
scenarios: An operator copies a flat-config example verbatim (substituting a real secret) and commits the config file to their repository — the doc example normalized this pattern by not stating that secret fields must come from environment variables or a vault.
An operator deploying the `introspection` verifier puts a live `client_secret` inline in `rebar.toml` — the guide did not instruct otherwise, so the secret is exposed in any repo snapshot or backup.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `docs/mcp-auth.md` AC (AC1) and in the What bullet describing the five verifier examples, add: 'Each example that includes a secret field (signing key, client_secret, token value, upstream credential) MUST use a sentinel placeholder (e.g. `<YOUR_SECRET>` or an env-var interpolation reference) and an inline note such as: "Never commit this value — inject via environment variable or a secrets manager."'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan (What / Approach) an explicit pointer to the config struct, schema file, or module (introduced in S1–S6) that is the authoritative source of the `auth_strategies` closed vocabulary, so the S7 implementer can verify the five-mode enumeration is complete before publishing the guide.
evidence: Plan What: 'all five verifier modes (`static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom`) with a flat-config example each, and the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary'
AC: 'all five verifier modes with a flat-config example each + the `auth_strategies` vocabulary + the one-instance-per-type limitation'
Zero matches for `auth_strategies` in src/ and docs/ — the symbol does not exist in the current codebase and the plan provides no pointer to where S1-S6 will define it
ABSENCE: no config schema file, dataclass, or TOML key specification cited for the doc author to cross-check
findings.finding: The plan names `auth_strategies` as a 'closed vocabulary' that `docs/mcp-auth.md` must document, and the AC requires the guide cover 'the `auth_strategies` vocabulary + the one-instance-per-type limitation', but nowhere in the plan (nor in the currently searchable codebase) is the canonical source of truth for this vocabulary identified — no config struct, schema file, or module path is cited for the implementer of this docs story to consult and verify completeness.
findings.location: What section — 'the closed `auth_strategies` vocabulary'; AC line 1 — '`auth_strategies` vocabulary'
scenarios: The doc author, implementing S7, cannot verify they have enumerated the correct and complete set of strategy type keys because no authoritative schema or module is named.
If S1–S6 add a sixth mode or rename a mode, the S7 doc author has no pointed location to diff against.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'The `auth_strategies` vocabulary and its five mode keys are defined in `<module>` (S1–S6); the S7 doc author MUST diff `docs/mcp-auth.md` against that struct before merge to confirm completeness.' Replace `<module>` with the actual path once S1–S6 land.
